questions.stem: The organism shown is a fastidious gram-negative coccobacillus that will not grow unless the culture medium is enriched. Which two accessory factors, released from lysed red blood cells, does it require?

explanation.summary: H. influenzae cannot lyse RBCs itself and requires exogenous factor X (hematin) and factor V (NAD), which is why it grows only on media containing lysed blood, such as chocolate agar.
explanation.whyCorrect: H. influenzae cannot lyse RBCs itself and requires exogenous factor X (hematin) and factor V (NAD), which is why it grows only on media containing lysed blood, such as chocolate agar.

questions.stem: H. influenzae grows on the brown agar plate shown but fails to grow on standard sheep blood agar. Why does this brown medium support its growth?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It neutralizes the IgA protease of the organism

choices.label: B
choices.text: It contains antibiotics that selectively favor Haemophilus

choices.label: C
choices.text: Heating lyses the red blood cells, releasing factors X and V that H. influenzae cannot obtain on its own

choices.label: D
choices.text: It supplies the 5% CO2 required for growth

choices.label: E
choices.text: It provides mannitol as a fermentable carbon source

explanation.summary: The agar is browned by heating blood, which lyses RBCs and frees factor X (hematin) and factor V (NAD). On intact blood agar these factors remain inside RBCs, so H. influenzae cannot grow.
explanation.whyCorrect: The agar is browned by heating blood, which lyses RBCs and frees factor X (hematin) and factor V (NAD). On intact blood agar these factors remain inside RBCs, so H. influenzae cannot grow.

questions.stem: This ventral surface of the brain is covered by a thick purulent exudate over the meninges. This gross finding is most characteristic of which condition?

explanation.summary: A dense neutrophil-rich purulent exudate blanketing the meninges is the hallmark of acute pyogenic bacterial meningitis, as caused by H. influenzae or N. meningitidis; viral meningitis produces a lymphocytic, non-purulent picture.
explanation.whyCorrect: A dense neutrophil-rich purulent exudate blanketing the meninges is the hallmark of acute pyogenic bacterial meningitis, as caused by H. influenzae or N. meningitidis; viral meningitis produces a lymphocytic, non-purulent picture.
